    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 84
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/84](https://www.erdosproblems.com/84)
    23→
    24→The cycle set of a graph $G$ on $n$ vertices is the set $A \subseteq \{3, \ldots, n\}$ of all cycle
    25→lengths present in $G$. Let $f(n)$ count the number of distinct such sets $A$ over all graphs on
    26→$n$ vertices. Erdős and Faudree conjectured that $f(n) = o(2^n)$ and $f(n) / 2^{n/2} \to \infty$.
    27→
    28→Erdős and Faudree showed $2^{n/2} < f(n) \leq 2^{n-2}$.
    29→
    30→[Er94b] Erdős, P., _Some old and new problems in various branches of combinatorics_.
    31→Discrete Math. 165/166 (1997), 227–231.
    32→
    33→[Er95] Erdős, P., _Some recent problems and results in graph theory_.
    34→Discrete Math. 164 (1997), 81–85.
    35→
    36→[Er96] Erdős, P., _Some of my favourite problems in various branches of combinatorics_.
    37→Matematiche (Catania) 47 (1992), 231–240.
    38→
    39→[Er97d] Erdős, P., _Some problems and results in combinatorial number theory_.
    40→
    41→[Ve04] Verstraëte, J., _On arithmetic progressions of cycle lengths in graphs_.
    42→Combin. Probab. Comput. 9 (2000), 369–373.
    43→-/
    44→
    45→open SimpleGraph
    46→
    47→namespace Erdos84
    48→
    49→/-- The cycle spectrum of a simple graph: the set of all cycle lengths present in $G$. -/
    50→def cycleSpectrum {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) : Set ℕ :=
    51→  {ℓ : ℕ | ∃ v : V, ∃ p : G.Walk v v, p.IsCycle ∧ p.length = ℓ}
    52→
    53→/-- The number of distinct cycle spectra realizable by simple graphs on $\operatorname{Fin}(n)$. -/
    54→noncomputable def cycleSetCount (n : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    55→  Set.ncard {A : Set ℕ | ∃ G : SimpleGraph (Fin n), cycleSpectrum G = A}
    56→
    57→/--
    58→Erdős Problem 84, Part 2 (open) [Er94b][Er95][Er96][Er97d]:
    59→$f(n) / 2^{n/2} \to \infty$.
    60→That is, for every $B > 0$, for all sufficiently large $n$, $f(n) \geq B \cdot 2^{n/2}$.
    61→-/
    62→@[category research open, AMS 5]
    63→theorem erdos_84 :
    64→    ∀ B : ℝ, B > 0 →
    65→    ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, N ≤ n →
    66→      (cycleSetCount n : ℝ) ≥ B * (2 : ℝ) ^ ((n : ℝ) / 2) := by
    67→  sorry
    68→
    69→/--
    70→Erdős Problem 84, Part 1 (proved by Verstraëte [Ve04]):
    71→$f(n) = o(2^n)$.
    72→That is, for every $\varepsilon > 0$, for all sufficiently large $n$,
    73→$f(n) \leq \varepsilon \cdot 2^n$.
    74→
    75→Verstraëte proved $f(n) \ll 2^{n - n^{1/10}}$, improved by Nenadov to
    76→$f(n) \ll 2^{n - n^{1/2 - o(1)}}$.
    77→-/
    78→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    79→theorem erdos_84.variants.subexponential :
    80→    ∀ ε : ℝ, ε > 0 →
    81→    ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, N ≤ n →
    82→      (cycleSetCount n : ℝ) ≤ ε * (2 : ℝ) ^ n := by
    83→  sorry
    84→
    85→end Erdos84
    86→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 752
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/752](https://www.erdosproblems.com/752)
    23→
    24→A question of Erdős, Faudree, and Schelp. Let $G$ be a graph with minimum degree $k$ and
    25→girth $> 2s$. Must there be $\gg k^s$ many distinct cycle lengths in $G$?
    26→
    27→Erdős, Faudree, and Schelp proved it when $s = 2$.
    28→
    29→[SuVe08] Sudakov, B. and Verstraëte, J., *Cycle lengths in sparse graphs*. Combinatorica 28
    30→(2008), 357–372.
    31→-/
    32→
    33→open SimpleGraph
    34→
    35→namespace Erdos752
    36→
    37→/-- The set of cycle lengths occurring in a simple graph. -/
    38→def cycleLengths {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) : Set ℕ :=
    39→  {n | ∃ (v : V) (p : G.Walk v v), p.IsCycle ∧ p.length = n}
    40→
    41→/--
    42→**Sudakov–Verstraëte Theorem (Erdős Problem 752)** [SuVe08]:
    43→
    44→There exists an absolute constant $c > 0$ such that for every finite graph $G$
    45→with minimum degree at least $k$ and girth greater than $2s$, the number of
    46→distinct cycle lengths in $G$ is at least $c \cdot k^s$.
    47→-/
    48→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    49→theorem erdos_752 : answer(True) ↔
    50→    ∃ c : ℝ, c > 0 ∧
    51→    ∀ (V : Type*) [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V]
    52→      (G : SimpleGraph V) [DecidableRel G.Adj]
    53→      (k s : ℕ),
    54→      (∀ v : V, k ≤ G.degree v) →
    55→      (∀ (v : V) (p : G.Walk v v), p.IsCycle → 2 * s < p.length) →
    56→      c * (k : ℝ) ^ s ≤ ((cycleLengths G).ncard : ℝ) := by
    57→  sorry
    58→
    59→end Erdos752
    60→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 751
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/751](https://www.erdosproblems.com/751)
    23→
    24→Let $G$ be a graph with chromatic number $\chi(G) = 4$. If $m_1 < m_2 < \cdots$ are the
    25→lengths of the cycles in $G$, can $\min(m_{i+1} - m_i)$ be arbitrarily large?
    26→
    27→The answer is no: Bondy and Vince [BoVi98] proved that every graph with minimum degree
    28→at least $3$ has two cycles whose lengths differ by at most $2$, and hence the same is
    29→true for every graph with chromatic number $4$.
    30→
    31→[BoVi98] Bondy, J. A. and Vince, A., _Cycles in a graph whose lengths differ by one or
    32→two_. Journal of Graph Theory (1998), 11-15.
    33→-/
    34→
    35→open SimpleGraph
    36→
    37→namespace Erdos751
    38→
    39→/-- The set of cycle lengths occurring in a simple graph. -/
    40→def cycleLengths {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) : Set ℕ :=
    41→  {n | ∃ (v : V) (p : G.Walk v v), p.IsCycle ∧ p.length = n}
    42→
    43→/-- **Erdős Problem 751**: If $G$ is a graph with $\chi(G) = 4$ and $m_1 < m_2 < \cdots$
    44→are the cycle lengths of $G$, the minimum gap $\min(m_{i+1} - m_i)$ cannot be made
    45→arbitrarily large. Bondy and Vince [BoVi98] proved that every graph with minimum degree
    46→at least $3$ has two cycles whose lengths differ by at most $2$, which implies the
    47→negative answer since $\chi(G) = 4$ forces a subgraph with minimum degree $\geq 3$.
    48→
    49→[BoVi98] -/
    50→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    51→theorem erdos_751 : answer(False) ↔
    52→    (∀ C : ℕ, ∃ (V : Type) (_ : Fintype V) (G : SimpleGraph V),
    53→      G.chromaticNumber = 4 ∧
    54→        ∀ m₁ ∈ cycleLengths G, ∀ m₂ ∈ cycleLengths G,
    55→          m₁ < m₂ → C ≤ m₂ - m₁) := by
    56→  sorry
    57→
    58→/-- **Bondy–Vince Theorem**: Every graph with minimum degree at least $3$ contains two
    59→cycles whose lengths differ by at most $2$. This is the stronger result that resolves
    60→Erdős Problem 751.
    61→
    62→[BoVi98] -/
    63→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    64→theorem erdos_751.variants.bondy_vince {V : Type*} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V]
    65→    (G : SimpleGraph V) [DecidableRel G.Adj]
    66→    (hmin : ∀ v : V, 3 ≤ G.degree v) :
    67→    ∃ m₁ ∈ cycleLengths G, ∃ m₂ ∈ cycleLengths G,
    68→      m₁ < m₂ ∧ m₂ - m₁ ≤ 2 := by
    69→  sorry
    70→
    71→end Erdos751

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 65
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/65](https://www.erdosproblems.com/65)
    23→
    24→Is the sum of reciprocals of distinct cycle lengths in a graph on $n$ vertices with $kn$
    25→edges minimised by the complete bipartite graph? Gyárfás, Komlós, and Szemerédi [GKS84]
    26→proved $\sum 1/a_i \gg \log k$; Liu and Montgomery [LiMo20] proved the sharp bound.
    27→
    28→[GKS84] Gyárfás, A., Komlós, J., and Szemerédi, E., *On the distribution of cycle lengths in
    29→graphs*, J. Graph Theory 8 (1984), 441–462.
    30→
    31→[LiMo20] Liu, H. and Montgomery, R., *A solution to Erdős and Hajnal's odd cycle problem*,
    32→J. Amer. Math. Soc. 36 (2023), 1191–1234.
    33→-/
    34→
    35→open SimpleGraph Finset
    36→
    37→namespace Erdos65
    38→
    39→/-- The complete bipartite graph $K_{a,b}$ on `Fin (a + b)`, where vertices
    40→$\{0, \ldots, a-1\}$ form one part and $\{a, \ldots, a+b-1\}$ form the other. -/
    41→def completeBipartiteGraph65 (a b : ℕ) : SimpleGraph (Fin (a + b)) where
    42→  Adj u v := (u.val < a ∧ a ≤ v.val) ∨ (a ≤ u.val ∧ v.val < a)
    43→  symm u v h := by
    44→    rcases h with ⟨hu, hv⟩ | ⟨hu, hv⟩
    45→    · exact Or.inr ⟨hv, hu⟩
    46→    · exact Or.inl ⟨hv, hu⟩
    47→  loopless v h := by rcases h with ⟨h1, h2⟩ | ⟨h1, h2⟩ <;> omega
    48→
    49→/--
    50→Erdős Problem #65 (Erdős–Hajnal) [GKS84] [LiMo20]:
    51→Let $G$ be a graph with $n$ vertices and $kn$ edges, and let $a_1 < a_2 < \cdots$ be the
    52→distinct lengths of cycles in $G$. Is it true that $\sum 1/a_i \gg \log k$? Is the sum
    53→$\sum 1/a_i$ minimised when $G$ is a complete bipartite graph?
    54→
    55→The first question was proved by Gyárfás, Komlós, and Szemerédi [GKS84].
    56→Liu and Montgomery [LiMo20] proved the asymptotically sharp lower bound
    57→$\geq (1/2 - o(1)) \log k$.
    58→
    59→The remaining open question is formalized below: for any graph $G$ on $n$ vertices whose edge
    60→count equals $a \cdot b$ for some partition $a + b = n$, the sum of reciprocals of distinct cycle
    61→lengths of $G$ is at least the corresponding sum for the complete bipartite graph $K_{a,b}$.
    62→-/
    63→@[category research open, AMS 5]
    64→theorem erdos_65 : answer(sorry) ↔
    65→    ∀ (n : ℕ) (G : SimpleGraph (Fin n)) (a b : ℕ), a + b = n →
    66→      ∀ [DecidableRel G.Adj], a * b = G.edgeFinset.card →
    67→      ∀ (T_G : Finset ℕ),
    68→        (∀ m ∈ T_G, ∃ v : Fin n, ∃ p : G.Walk v v, p.IsCycle ∧ p.length = m) →
    69→        (∀ m : ℕ, (∃ v : Fin n, ∃ p : G.Walk v v, p.IsCycle ∧ p.length = m) → m ∈ T_G) →
    70→        ∃ (T_K : Finset ℕ),
    71→          (∀ m ∈ T_K, ∃ v : Fin (a + b),
    72→            ∃ p : (completeBipartiteGraph65 a b).Walk v v, p.IsCycle ∧ p.length = m) ∧
    73→          (∀ m : ℕ, (∃ v : Fin (a + b),
    74→            ∃ p : (completeBipartiteGraph65 a b).Walk v v, p.IsCycle ∧ p.length = m) → m ∈ T_K) ∧
    75→          ∑ m ∈ T_K, (1 / (m : ℝ)) ≤ ∑ m ∈ T_G, (1 / (m : ℝ)) := by
    76→  sorry
    77→
    78→end Erdos65
    79→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 58
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/58](https://www.erdosproblems.com/58)
    23→
    24→Conjectured by Bollobás and Erdős. Bollobás and Shelah confirmed it for $k = 1$.
    25→Proved by Gyárfás [Gy92], who showed the stronger result that if $G$ is 2-connected,
    26→then $G$ is either $K_{2k+2}$ or contains a vertex of degree at most $2k$.
    27→
    28→[Gy92] Gyárfás, A., *Graphs with $k$ odd cycle lengths*, Discrete Mathematics 103 (1992), 41–48.
    29→-/
    30→
    31→open SimpleGraph
    32→
    33→namespace Erdos58
    34→
    35→/-- The set of lengths of odd cycles in a graph $G$. -/
    36→def oddCycleLengths {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) : Set ℕ :=
    37→  {n : ℕ | Odd n ∧ ∃ (v : V) (p : G.Walk v v), p.IsCycle ∧ p.length = n}
    38→
    39→/--
    40→**Erdős Problem 58** (Bollobás–Erdős conjecture, proved by Gyárfás [Gy92]):
    41→
    42→If $G$ is a finite graph containing odd cycles of at most $k$ different lengths,
    43→then $\chi(G) \leq 2k + 2$, with equality if and only if $G$ contains $K_{2k+2}$ as a
    44→clique subgraph.
    45→-/
    46→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    47→theorem erdos_58 {V : Type*} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V]
    48→    (G : SimpleGraph V) [DecidableRel G.Adj] (k : ℕ)
    49→    (hk : (oddCycleLengths G).ncard ≤ k) :
    50→    G.chromaticNumber ≤ (2 * k + 2 : ℕ) ∧
    51→    (G.chromaticNumber = (2 * k + 2 : ℕ) ↔ ∃ s : Finset V, G.IsNClique (2 * k + 2) s) := by
    52→  sorry
    53→
    54→end Erdos58

    16→import FormalConjecturesForMathlib.Combinatorics.SimpleGraph.GraphConjectures.Definitions
    17→import FormalConjecturesForMathlib.Combinatorics.SimpleGraph.GraphConjectures.Domination
    18→import Mathlib.Analysis.Matrix.Spectrum
    19→import Mathlib.Combinatorics.SimpleGraph.AdjMatrix
    20→import Mathlib.Combinatorics.SimpleGraph.Metric
    21→import Mathlib.Data.Multiset.Interval
    22→
    23→noncomputable def Matrix.IsHermitian.maxEigenvalue {𝕜 : Type*} [Field 𝕜] [RCLike 𝕜]
    24→    {n : Type*} [Fintype n] [DecidableEq n] {A : Matrix n n 𝕜} (hA : A.IsHermitian) : ℝ :=
    25→  iSup hA.eigenvalues
    26→
    27→namespace SimpleGraph
    28→
    29→open Classical
    30→
    31→variable {α : Type*} [Fintype α] [DecidableEq α]
    32→
    33→/-- Abbreviation for the average independence number of the neighborhoods.
    34→-/
    35→noncomputable abbrev l (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℝ := averageIndepNeighbors G
    36→
    37→/-- The same quantity under a different name, used in some conjectures.
    38→-/
    39→noncomputable abbrev l_avg (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℝ := averageIndepNeighbors G
    40→
    41→/-- Independent domination number of `G`. -/
    42→noncomputable def gi (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℕ := G.indepDominationNumber
    43→
    44→/-- `temp_v G v = deg(v)/(n(G) - deg(v))`. -/
    45→noncomputable def temp_v (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] (v : α) : ℝ :=
    46→  let n := Fintype.card α
    47→  let deg := G.degree v
    48→  if n = deg then 0 else (deg : ℝ) / ((n : ℝ) - (deg : ℝ))
    49→
    50→/-- Maximum of `temp_v` over all vertices. -/
    51→noncomputable def MaxTemp (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] [Fintype α] [Nonempty α] : ℝ :=
    52→  let temps := Finset.univ.image (temp_v G)
    53→  temps.max' (Finset.image_nonempty.mpr Finset.univ_nonempty)
    54→
    55→/-- Cardinality of the union of the neighbourhoods of the ends of the non-edge `e`. -/
    56→def non_edge_neighborhood_card (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] (e : Sym2 α) : ℕ :=
    57→  Sym2.lift ⟨fun u v => (G.neighborFinset u ∪ G.neighborFinset v).card,
    58→    fun u v => by simp [Finset.union_comm]⟩ e
    59→
    60→/-- Minimum size of the neighbourhood of a non-edge of `G`. -/
    61→noncomputable def NG (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℝ :=
    62→  let non_edges := (compl G).edgeFinset
    63→  if h : non_edges.Nonempty then
    64→    let neighbor_sizes := non_edges.image (non_edge_neighborhood_card G)
    65→    (neighbor_sizes.min' (Finset.Nonempty.image h _))
    66→  else
    67→    (Fintype.card α : ℝ)
    68→
    69→/-- Size of a largest induced forest of `G` expressed as a real number. -/
    70→noncomputable def S (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℝ :=
    71→  let card := Fintype.card α
    72→  if card < 2 then 0 else
    73→    let degrees := Multiset.ofList (List.map (fun v => G.degree v) Finset.univ.toList)
    74→    let sorted_degrees := degrees.sort (· ≤ ·)
    75→    ↑((sorted_degrees[card - 2]?).getD 0)
    76→
    77→/-- Local eccentricity of a vertex. -/
    78→noncomputable def local_eccentricity (G : SimpleGraph α) (v : α) : ENat :=
    79→  sSup (Set.range (G.edist v))
    80→
    81→/-- The largest integer less than or equal to `x`. -/
    82→noncomputable def FLOOR (x : ℝ) : ℝ := ⌊x⌋
    83→
    84→/-- Eccentricity of a vertex. -/
    85→noncomputable def eccentricity (G : SimpleGraph α) (v : α) : ℕ∞ :=
    86→  sSup (Set.range (G.edist v))
    87→
    88→/-- The minimum eccentricity among all vertices. -/
    89→noncomputable def minEccentricity (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℕ∞ :=
    90→  sInf (Set.range (eccentricity G))
    91→
    92→/-- The set of vertices of minimum eccentricity. -/
    93→noncomputable def graphCenter (G : SimpleGraph α) : Set α :=
    94→  {v : α | eccentricity G v = minEccentricity G}
    95→
    96→/-- The maximum eccentricity among all vertices. -/
    97→noncomputable def maxEccentricity (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℕ∞ :=
    98→  sSup (Set.range (eccentricity G))
    99→
   100→/-- The set of vertices of maximum eccentricity. -/
   101→noncomputable def maxEccentricityVertices (G : SimpleGraph α) : Set α :=
   102→  {v : α | eccentricity G v = maxEccentricity G}
   103→
   104→/-- Distance from a vertex to a finite set. -/
   105→noncomputable def distToSet (G : SimpleGraph α) (v : α) (S : Set α) : ℕ :=
   106→  if h : S.toFinset.Nonempty then
   107→    (S.toFinset.image (fun s => G.dist v s)).min' (Finset.Nonempty.image h _)
   108→  else 0
   109→
   110→/-- Average distance of `G`. -/
   111→noncomputable def averageDistance (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℝ :=
   112→  if Fintype.card α > 1 then
   113→    (∑ u ∈ Finset.univ, ∑ v ∈ Finset.univ, (G.dist u v : ℝ)) /
   114→      ((Fintype.card α : ℝ) * ((Fintype.card α : ℝ) - 1))
   115→  else
   116→    0
   117→
   118→/-- The floor of the average distance of `G`. -/
   119→noncomputable def path (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℕ :=
   120→  if G.Connected then
   121→    Nat.floor (averageDistance G)
   122→  else
   123→    0
   124→
   125→/-- Auxiliary quantity `ecc` used in conjecture 34. -/
   126→noncomputable def ecc (G : SimpleGraph α) (S : Set α) : ℕ :=
   127→  let s_comp := Finset.univ.filter (fun v => v ∉ S)
   128→  if h : s_comp.Nonempty then
   129→    (s_comp.image (fun v => distToSet G v S)).max' (Finset.Nonempty.image h _)
   130→  else 0
   131→
   132→/-- Average distance from all vertices to a given set. -/
   133→noncomputable def distavg (G : SimpleGraph α) (S : Set α) : ℝ :=
   134→  if Fintype.card α > 0 then
   135→    (∑ v ∈ Finset.univ, (distToSet G v S : ℝ)) / (Fintype.card α : ℝ)
   136→  else
   137→    0
   138→
   139→/-- A family of paths covering all vertices without overlaps. -/
   140→def IsPathCover (G : SimpleGraph α) (P : Finset (Finset α)) : Prop :=
   141→  (∀ s1 ∈ P, ∀ s2 ∈ P, s1 ≠ s2 → Disjoint s1 s2) ∧
   142→  (Finset.univ ⊆ P.biUnion id) ∧
   143→  (∀ s ∈ P, ∃ (u v : α) (p : G.Walk u v), p.IsPath ∧ s = p.support.toFinset)
   144→
   145→/-- Minimum size of a path cover of `G`. -/
   146→noncomputable def pathCoverNumber (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℕ :=
   147→  sInf { k | ∃ P : Finset (Finset α), P.card = k ∧ IsPathCover G P }
   148→
   149→/-- The same quantity as a real number. -/
   150→noncomputable def p (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℝ := (pathCoverNumber G : ℝ)
   151→
   152→/-- The Wiener index of `G`, which is the sum of distances between all
   153→pairs of vertices. -/
   154→noncomputable def wienerIndex (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℕ :=
   155→  ∑ uv : Sym2 α, uv.lift ⟨fun u v ↦ G.dist u v, by simp [dist_comm]⟩
   156→
   157→/-- Auxiliary function for Szeged index: counts vertices closer to u than v. -/
   158→noncomputable def szeged_aux (G : SimpleGraph α) (u v : α) : ℕ :=
   159→  (Finset.univ.filter (fun w => G.edist w u < G.edist w v)).card
   160→
   161→/-- The Szeged index of `G`.
   162→
   163→This is define as the sum `∑_{uv ∈ E(G)} n_u(u,v) * n_v(u,v)` where
   164→`n_u(uv)` is the number of vertices closer to `u` than `v`.
   165→-/
   166→noncomputable def szegedIndex (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℕ :=
   167→  ∑ e ∈ G.edgeFinset,
   168→    e.lift ⟨fun u v => szeged_aux G u v * szeged_aux G v u, by simp [mul_comm]⟩
   169→
   170→/-- The average degree of `G`. -/
   171→noncomputable def averageDegree (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℚ  :=
   172→  (∑ v, (G.degree v : ℚ)) / (Fintype.card α : ℚ)
   173→
   174→/-- The multiset of degrees of a graph. -/
   175→def degreeMultiset (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : Multiset ℕ :=
   176→  Finset.univ.val.map fun v => G.degree v
   177→
   178→/-- The annihilation number of a graph. This is the largest number of degrees that can be added
   179→together without going over the total number of edges of that graph. -/
   180→def annihilationNumber (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℕ :=
   181→  -- Calculate the limit: The number of edges (Sum of degrees / 2)
   182→  letI limit := G.edgeFinset.card
   183→
   184→  -- The set of all multisets of degrees that sum to less than or equal to `limit`
   185→  Finset.Iic (degreeMultiset G)
   186→    |>.filter (fun S ↦ Multiset.sum S ≤ limit)
   187→    |>.sup Multiset.card
   188→
   189→/--
   190→Computes the annihilation number of a graph G.
   191→
   192→It calculates the degree sequence, sorts it ascendingly, and finds the largest prefix length 'k'
   193→(where `0 ≤ k ≤ |V(G)|`) such that the sum of the prefix is less than or equal to the sum of the corresponding suffix.
   194→-/
   195→noncomputable def annihilationNumber' (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℕ :=
   196→  -- 1. Get the degree sequence sorted in ascending order.
   197→  -- G.degree_list returns the list of degrees.
   198→  letI degrees := (Finset.univ.image fun v => G.degree v).sort (· ≤ ·)
   199→
   200→  -- 2. Define the condition for the annihilation number.
   201→  -- k represents the number of smallest degrees considered (the length of the prefix).
   202→  letI condition (k : ℕ) : Bool := (degrees.take k).sum ≤ (degrees.drop k).sum
   203→
   204→  -- 3. Find the maximum k in {0, ..., n} satisfying the condition.
   205→  -- List.range (n + 1) generates the list [0, 1, ..., n].
   206→  letI candidates := (List.range (Fintype.card α + 1)).filter condition
   207→
   208→  -- 4. Return the maximum candidate.
   209→  -- The list of candidates is guaranteed to be non-empty because k=0 always satisfies
   210→  -- the condition (0 ≤ sum of all degrees).
   211→  candidates.getLast!
   212→
   213→set_option linter.unusedSectionVars false in
   214→-- TODO(Paul-Lez): debug the issue with the unused variable linter...
   215→proof_wanted annihilationNumberEq (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] :
   216→    annihilationNumber G = annihilationNumber' G
   217→
   218→/-!
   219→## Residue
   220→The residue of a graph is the number of zeros remaining after iteratively applying the Havel-Hakimi
   221→algorithm to the degree sequence until all remaining degrees are zero.
   222→-/
   223→
   224→/--
   225→Helper function: Performs one step of the Havel-Hakimi reduction on a degree sequence.
   226→Assumes the input list `s` is sorted descending.
   227→Removes the first element `d`, decrements the next `d` elements by 1, and re-sorts the list descending.
   228→
   229→Note: when `s` is the list of vertices arising from a simple graph, if the first index is `s` then
   230→the degree list always has length at least `s+1` so this makes sense.
   231→-/
   232→private def havelHakimiStep (s : List ℕ) : List ℕ :=
   233→  match s with
   234→  | [] => []
   235→  | d :: rest =>
   236→    -- Split the rest into the part to decrement (first d elements) and the remaining part.
   237→    let (to_decrement, remaining) := rest.splitAt d
   238→    -- Decrement the elements
   239→    let decremented := to_decrement.map (· - 1)
   240→    -- Combine and re-sort descending.
   241→    (decremented ++ remaining).mergeSort (· ≥ ·)
   242→
   243→/--
   244→Auxiliary function to calculate the residue recursively.
   245→Applies Havel-Hakimi steps until the sequence consists only of zeros or is empty.
   246→-/
   247→private partial def residueAux : List ℕ → ℕ
   248→  | [] => 0        -- Empty sequence, residue is 0.
   249→  | 0 :: s => 1 + s.length -- If the largest degree is 0 (and the list is sorted), all are 0.
   250→  | s => residueAux (havelHakimiStep s) -- Apply one reduction step and recurse.
   251→
   252→/--
   253→Computes the residue of a graph G, ,i.e. the number of zeros remaining after iteratively applying the Havel-Hakimi
   254→algorithm to the degree sequence until all remaining degrees are zero.
   255→Starts with the descending degree sequence and applies the Havel-Hakimi process.
   256→-/
   257→noncomputable def residue (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℕ :=
   258→  -- Get the degree sequence sorted in descending order and apply `residueAux`.
   259→  residueAux ((Finset.univ.image fun v => G.degree v).sort (· ≥ ·))
   260→
   261→/--
   262→Fractional alpha. This is defined as
   263→$$
   264→\max_x \sum_{v \in V} x_v
   265→$$
   266→subject to $0 \le x_v \le 1$ for all $v \in V$ and
   267→$x_u + x_v \le 1$ whenever $(u, v)$ are connected by an edge.
   268→-/
   269→noncomputable def fractionalAlpha (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℝ :=
   270→  sSup {(∑ i, x i) | (x : α → ℝ) (_ : ∀ v, x v ∈ Set.Icc 0 1)
   271→    (_ : ∀ u v, G.Adj u v → x u + x v ≤ 1)}
   272→
   273→/--
   274→Lovász Theta Function (ϑ(G))
   275→The Lovász theta function is defined as:
   276→ϑ(G) = min λ_max(A)
   277→where the minimum is taken over all symmetric matrices A such that:
   278→
   279→A_ij = 1 for all i = j (diagonal entries are 1)
   280→A_ij = 0 for all {i,j} ∈ E (entries corresponding to edges are 0)
   281→A is positive semidefinite
   282→
   283→Here λ_max(A) denotes the maximum eigenvalue of A.
   284→-/
   285→noncomputable def lovaczThetaFunction
   286→    (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℝ :=
   287→  sInf {(Matrix.IsHermitian.maxEigenvalue hA) | (A : Matrix α α ℝ) (hA : A.IsHermitian)
   288→      (_ : ∀ i, A i i = 1) (_ : ∀ i j, G.Adj i j → A i j = 0)}
   289→
   290→/--
   291→Given a simple graph `G` with adjacency matrix `A`, this is the number `n₀ + min n₊ n₋` where:
   292→- `n₀` is the multiplicity of zero as an eigenvalue of `A`
   293→- `n₊` is the number of positive eigenvalues of `A` (counting multiplicities)
   294→- `n₋` is the number of negative eigenvalues of `A` (counting multiplicities)
   295→-/
   296→noncomputable def cvetkovic (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℕ :=
   297→  letI A : Matrix α α ℝ := G.adjMatrix ℝ
   298→  letI spectrum : Multiset ℝ := (Matrix.charpoly A).roots
   299→  letI positive_count : ℕ := spectrum.countP (fun x => 0 < x)
   300→  letI negative_count : ℕ := spectrum.countP (fun x => 0 > x)
   301→  letI zero_count : ℕ := spectrum.countP (fun x => 0 = x)
   302→  zero_count + min positive_count negative_count
   303→
   304→end SimpleGraph
   305→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 802
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/802](https://www.erdosproblems.com/802)
    23→
    24→A conjecture of Ajtai, Erdős, Komlós, and Szemerédi [AEKS81], who proved a
    25→lower bound of $\gg_r (\log \log(t+1) / t) \cdot n$. Shearer [Sh95] improved this to
    26→$\gg_r (\log t / (\log \log(t+1) \cdot t)) \cdot n$. Ajtai, Komlós, and Szemerédi [AKS80]
    27→proved the conjectured bound when $r = 3$. Alon [Al96b] proved the conjectured
    28→bound under the stronger assumption that the induced graph on every vertex
    29→neighbourhood has chromatic number $\leq r - 2$.
    30→-/
    31→
    32→open SimpleGraph Finset
    33→
    34→namespace Erdos802
    35→
    36→/-- The average degree of a finite simple graph on `Fin n`. -/
    37→noncomputable def avgDegree {n : ℕ}
    38→    (G : SimpleGraph (Fin n)) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℝ :=
    39→  (∑ v : Fin n, (G.degree v : ℝ)) / (n : ℝ)
    40→
    41→/--
    42→Erdős Problem 802 [AEKS81]:
    43→
    44→For every $r \geq 3$, there exists a constant $c > 0$ (depending on $r$) such that
    45→every $K_r$-free graph $G$ on $n$ vertices with average degree $t \geq 2$ contains an
    46→independent set of size at least $c \cdot (\log t / t) \cdot n$.
    47→-/
    48→@[category research open, AMS 5]
    49→theorem erdos_802 : answer(sorry) ↔
    50→    ∀ (r : ℕ), r ≥ 3 →
    51→    ∃ c : ℝ, c > 0 ∧
    52→    ∀ (n : ℕ) (G : SimpleGraph (Fin n)) [DecidableRel G.Adj],
    53→    G.CliqueFree r →
    54→    avgDegree G ≥ 2 →
    55→    ∃ S : Finset (Fin n),
    56→      (∀ u ∈ S, ∀ v ∈ S, u ≠ v → ¬G.Adj u v) ∧
    57→      (S.card : ℝ) ≥ c * (Real.log (avgDegree G) / avgDegree G) * (n : ℝ) := by
    58→  sorry
    59→
    60→end Erdos802

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 72
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/72](https://www.erdosproblems.com/72)
    23→
    24→There exists a density-zero set $A$ and a constant $c > 0$ such that every graph with
    25→average degree at least $c$ contains a cycle whose length is in $A$. Proved by
    26→Verstraëte [Ve05]; Liu and Montgomery [LiMo20] showed powers of $2$ work.
    27→
    28→[Ve05] Verstraëte, J., *A note on vertex-disjoint cycles*, Combinatorics, Probability and
    29→Computing 14 (2005), 127-143.
    30→
    31→[LiMo20] Liu, H. and Montgomery, R., *A solution to Erdős and Hajnal's odd cycle problem*,
    32→Journal of the American Mathematical Society 36 (2023), 1191-1234.
    33→-/
    34→
    35→open SimpleGraph
    36→
    37→namespace Erdos72
    38→
    39→/--
    40→A set $A \subseteq \mathbb{N}$ has natural density zero: for every $\varepsilon > 0$, for all
    41→sufficiently large $n$, $|A \cap \{0, \ldots, n\}| \leq \varepsilon \cdot n$.
    42→-/
    43→def HasDensityZero (A : Set ℕ) : Prop :=
    44→  ∀ ε : ℝ, ε > 0 → ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, n ≥ N →
    45→    (Set.ncard (A ∩ Set.Iic n) : ℝ) ≤ ε * (n : ℝ)
    46→
    47→/--
    48→Is there a set $A \subset \mathbb{N}$ of density $0$ and a constant $c > 0$ such that every graph on
    49→sufficiently many vertices with average degree $\geq c$ contains a cycle whose length is in $A$?
    50→
    51→Solved affirmatively by Verstraëte [Ve05] (non-constructive proof).
    52→Liu and Montgomery [LiMo20] proved this holds even when $A$ is the set of powers of $2$.
    53→-/
    54→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    55→theorem erdos_72 : answer(True) ↔
    56→    ∃ (A : Set ℕ), HasDensityZero A ∧
    57→    ∃ (c : ℝ), c > 0 ∧
    58→    ∃ (N₀ : ℕ), ∀ (n : ℕ), n ≥ N₀ →
    59→      ∀ (G : SimpleGraph (Fin n)) (hd : DecidableRel G.Adj),
    60→        haveI := hd
    61→        2 * (G.edgeFinset.card : ℝ) ≥ c * (n : ℝ) →
    62→        ∃ (k : ℕ), k ∈ A ∧ ∃ (v : Fin n), ∃ (p : G.Walk v v), p.IsCycle ∧ p.length = k := by
    63→  sorry
    64→
    65→end Erdos72

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 71
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/71](https://www.erdosproblems.com/71)
    23→
    24→For every infinite arithmetic progression containing even numbers, there exists $c > 0$
    25→such that every graph with average degree at least $c$ contains a cycle whose length
    26→belongs to the progression. Proved by Bollobás [Bo77].
    27→
    28→[Bo77] Bollobás, B., *Cycles modulo k*. Bull. London Math. Soc. 9 (1977), 97-98.
    29→-/
    30→
    31→open SimpleGraph
    32→
    33→namespace Erdos71
    34→
    35→/--
    36→Erdős Problem #71 (Erdős-Burr conjecture, proved by Bollobás [Bo77]):
    37→For every infinite arithmetic progression $P = \{a, a+d, a+2d, \ldots\}$ (with $d \geq 1$)
    38→that contains even numbers, there exists a constant $c = c(P) > 0$ such that every
    39→finite graph with average degree at least $c$ contains a cycle whose length belongs to $P$.
    40→
    41→The average degree of a graph $G$ on $n > 0$ vertices is $2|E(G)|/n$.
    42→-/
    43→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    44→theorem erdos_71 (a d : ℕ) (hd : 1 ≤ d) (heven : ∃ k : ℕ, Even (a + k * d)) :
    45→    ∃ c : ℝ, c > 0 ∧
    46→      ∀ (V : Type*) [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V] (G : SimpleGraph V) [DecidableRel G.Adj],
    47→        0 < Fintype.card V →
    48→        c ≤ (2 * (G.edgeFinset.card : ℝ)) / (Fintype.card V : ℝ) →
    49→        ∃ m : ℕ, (∃ k : ℕ, m = a + k * d) ∧
    50→          ∃ v : V, ∃ p : G.Walk v v, p.IsCycle ∧ p.length = m := by
    51→  sorry
    52→
    53→end Erdos71

    13→See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→import Mathlib.Combinatorics.SimpleGraph.Acyclic
    18→import Mathlib.Combinatorics.SimpleGraph.Bipartite
    19→import Mathlib.Combinatorics.SimpleGraph.Matching
    20→import Mathlib.Data.Real.Archimedean
    21→import Mathlib.Analysis.InnerProductSpace.PiL2
    22→
    23→namespace SimpleGraph
    24→
    25→open Classical Finset List
    26→
    27→variable {α : Type*} [Fintype α] [DecidableEq α]
    28→
    29→/-- `Ls G` is the maximum number of leaves over all spanning trees of `G`.
    30→It is defined to be `0` when `G` is not connected. -/
    31→noncomputable def Ls (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℝ :=
    32→  letI spanningTrees := { T : Subgraph G | T.IsSpanning ∧ IsTree T.coe }
    33→  letI leaves (T : Subgraph G) := T.verts.toFinset.filter (fun v => T.degree v = 1)
    34→  letI num_leaves (T : Subgraph G) := (leaves T).card
    35→  sSup (Set.image (fun T => (num_leaves T : ℝ)) spanningTrees)
    36→
    37→/-- `n G` is the number of vertices of `G` as a real number. -/
    38→noncomputable def n (_ : SimpleGraph α) : ℝ := Fintype.card α
    39→
    40→/-- `m G` is the size of a maximum matching of `G`. -/
    41→noncomputable def m (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℝ :=
    42→  let matchings := { M : Subgraph G | M.IsMatching }
    43→  sSup (Set.image (fun M => (M.edgeSet.toFinset.card : ℝ)) matchings)
    44→
    45→/-- The maximum cardinality among all independent sets `s`
    46→    that maximize the quantity `|s| - |N(s)|`, where `N(s)`
    47→    is the neighborhood of the set `s`. -/
    48→noncomputable def aprime (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℝ :=
    49→  letI indep_sets : Finset (Finset α) := univ.powerset.filter (fun s => G.IsIndepSet (s : Set α))
    50→  letI diff (s : Finset α) : ℤ := (s.card : ℤ) - (⋃ v ∈ (s : Set α), G.neighborSet v).toFinset.card
    51→  letI max_diff := (indep_sets.image diff).max
    52→  letI critical_sets := indep_sets.filter (fun s ↦ diff s = max_diff.getD 0)
    53→  letI max_card := (critical_sets.image Finset.card).max
    54→  (max_card.getD 0 : ℝ)
    55→
    56→/-- `largestInducedForestSize G` is the size of a largest induced forest of `G`. -/
    57→noncomputable def largestInducedForestSize (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℕ :=
    58→  sSup { n | ∃ s : Finset α, (G.induce s).IsAcyclic ∧ s.card = n }
    59→
    60→/-- The degree sequence of a graph, sorted in nondecreasing order. -/
    61→noncomputable def degreeSequence (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : List ℕ :=
    62→  (Finset.univ.val.map fun v : α => G.degree v).sort (· ≤ ·)
    63→
    64→/--
    65→The maximum number of occurrences of any term of the degree sequence of `G`.
    66→-/
    67→noncomputable def degreeSequenceMultiplicity (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℕ :=
    68→  letI degs := degreeSequence G
    69→  (List.max? (degs.map (fun d => degs.count d))).getD 0
    70→
    71→/-- `largestInducedBipartiteSubgraphSize G` is the size of a largest induced
    72→bipartite subgraph of `G`. -/
    73→noncomputable def largestInducedBipartiteSubgraphSize (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℕ :=
    74→  sSup { n | ∃ s : Finset α, (G.induce s).IsBipartite ∧ s.card = n }
    75→
    76→/-- `b G` is the number of vertices of a largest induced bipartite subgraph of `G`.
    77→Returned as a real number. -/
    78→noncomputable def b (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℝ :=
    79→  (largestInducedBipartiteSubgraphSize G : ℝ)
    80→
    81→/-- Independence number of the neighbourhood of `v`. -/
    82→noncomputable def indepNeighborsCard (G : SimpleGraph α) (v : α) : ℕ :=
    83→  (G.induce (G.neighborSet v)).indepNum
    84→
    85→/-- The same quantity as a real number. -/
    86→noncomputable def indepNeighbors (G : SimpleGraph α) (v : α) : ℝ :=
    87→  (indepNeighborsCard G v : ℝ)
    88→
    89→/-- Average of `indepNeighbors` over all vertices. -/
    90→noncomputable def averageIndepNeighbors (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℝ :=
    91→  (∑ v ∈ Finset.univ, indepNeighbors G v) / (Fintype.card α : ℝ)
    92→
    93→/-- A unit distance graph in ℝ²:
    94→A graph where the vertices V are a collection of points in ℝ² and there is
    95→an edge between two points if and only if the distance between them is 1. -/
    96→def UnitDistancePlaneGraph (V : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : SimpleGraph V where
    97→  Adj x y := Dist.dist x y = 1
    98→  symm x y := by simp [PseudoMetricSpace.dist_comm]
    99→
   100→/--
   101→Two walks are internally disjoint if they share no vertices other than their endpoints.
   102→-/
   103→def InternallyDisjoint {V : Type*} {G : SimpleGraph V} {u v x y : V}
   104→    (p : G.Walk u v) (q : G.Walk x y) : Prop :=
   105→  Disjoint p.support.tail.dropLast q.support.tail.dropLast
   106→
   107→/--
   108→We say a graph is infinitely connected if any two vertices are connected by infinitely many
   109→pairwise disjoint paths.
   110→-/
   111→def InfinitelyConnected {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) : Prop :=
   112→  Pairwise fun u v ↦ ∃ P : Set (G.Walk u v),
   113→    P.Infinite ∧ (∀ p ∈ P, p.IsPath) ∧ P.Pairwise InternallyDisjoint
   114→
   115→/-- Infinite graphs: definitions for max degree and clique number so that the maximum
   116→degree (resp. clique number) of a graph with unbounded degree (resp. clique size) is
   117→`∞` rather than 0.
   118→-/
   119→noncomputable
   120→def edegree {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) (v : V) : ℕ∞ := (G.neighborSet v).encard
   121→
   122→noncomputable
   123→def emaxDegree {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) : ℕ∞ := ⨆ v, G.edegree v
   124→
   125→noncomputable
   126→def ecliqueNum {V : Type} (G : SimpleGraph V) : ℕ∞ := ⨆ (s : Finset V) (_ : G.IsClique s), #s
   127→
   128→end SimpleGraph
   129→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1150
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1150](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1150)
    23→-/
    24→
    25→open scoped Polynomial
    26→
    27→namespace Erdos1150
    28→
    29→/--
    30→Is there some constant $c > 0$ such that, for all large enough $n$ and all polynomials $P$ of
    31→degree $n$ with coefficients in $\{-1, 1\}$,
    32→$$\max_{|z|=1} |P(z)| > (1 + c) \sqrt{n}?$$
    33→-/
    34→@[category research open, AMS 12 30]
    35→theorem erdos_1150 :
    36→    answer(sorry) ↔ ∃ c > 0, ∀ᶠ n in Filter.atTop,
    37→      ∀ P : ℂ[X],  (∀ i ≤ P.natDegree, P.coeff i = - 1 ∨ P.coeff i = 1) → P.natDegree = n →
    38→        ⨆ z : Metric.sphere (0 : ℂ) 1, ‖P.eval (z : ℂ)‖ > (1 + c) * Real.sqrt n := by
    39→  sorry
    40→
    41→/--
    42→The trivial lower bound from Parseval's identity: for any polynomial $P$ of degree $n$ with
    43→coefficients in $\{-1, 1\}$, we have $\max_{|z|=1} |P(z)| \geq \sqrt{n+1}$.
    44→
    45→This follows from Parseval's identity:
    46→$$\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_0^{2\pi} |P(e^{i\theta})|^2 d\theta = \sum_{k=0}^{n} |a_k|^2 = n+1$$
    47→since each $|a_k|^2 = 1$.
    48→-/
    49→@[category graduate, AMS 12 30]
    50→theorem erdos_1150.variants.parseval_lower_bound (P : ℂ[X]) (n : ℕ)
    51→    (hcoeff : ∀ i ≤ P.natDegree, P.coeff i = -1 ∨ P.coeff i = 1)
    52→    (hdeg : P.natDegree = n) :
    53→    ⨆ z : Metric.sphere (0 : ℂ) 1, ‖P.eval (z : ℂ)‖ ≥ Real.sqrt (n + 1) := by
    54→  sorry
    55→
    56→end Erdos1150
    57→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1133
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1133](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1133)
    23→
    24→Erdős proved that, for any $C > 0$, there exists $\varepsilon > 0$ such that if $n$ is sufficiently
    25→large and $m = \lfloor(1+\varepsilon)n\rfloor$ then for any $x_1, \ldots, x_m \in [-1,1]$ there is
    26→a polynomial $P$ of degree $n$ such that $|P(x_i)| \le 1$ for $1 \le i \le m$ and
    27→$\max_{x \in [-1,1]} |P(x)| > C$.
    28→The conjectured statement would also imply this, but Erdős said he could not even prove
    29→it for $m = n$.
    30→-/
    31→
    32→open Polynomial Finset Set
    33→
    34→namespace Erdos1133
    35→
    36→/--
    37→Erdős Problem 1133:
    38→Let $C > 0$. There exists $\varepsilon > 0$ such that for sufficiently large $n$, for any
    39→$x_1, \ldots, x_n \in [-1,1]$ there exist $y_1, \ldots, y_n \in [-1,1]$ such that any polynomial
    40→$P$ of degree $< (1+\varepsilon)n$ that agrees with $y_i$ at $x_i$ for at least $(1-\varepsilon)n$
    41→indices $i$ must satisfy $\max_{x \in [-1,1]} |P(x)| > C$.
    42→-/
    43→@[category research open, AMS 41]
    44→theorem erdos_1133 :
    45→    ∀ C : ℝ, C > 0 →
    46→    ∃ ε : ℝ, ε > 0 ∧
    47→    ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, N ≤ n →
    48→    ∀ x : Fin n → ℝ, (∀ i, x i ∈ Icc (-1 : ℝ) 1) →
    49→    ∃ y : Fin n → ℝ, (∀ i, y i ∈ Icc (-1 : ℝ) 1) ∧
    50→    ∀ P : Polynomial ℝ, (P.natDegree : ℝ) < (1 + ε) * n →
    51→    ((univ.filter (fun i : Fin n => P.eval (x i) = y i)).card : ℝ) ≥ (1 - ε) * n →
    52→    ∃ t ∈ Icc (-1 : ℝ) 1, |P.eval t| > C := by
    53→  sorry
    54→
    55→end Erdos1133
    56→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 612
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/612](https://www.erdosproblems.com/612)
    23→
    24→Let $G$ be a connected graph with $n$ vertices, minimum degree $d$, and diameter $D$.
    25→
    26→The original conjecture of Erdős, Pach, Pollack, and Tuza [EPPT89] stated:
    27→- If $G$ contains no $K_{2r}$ and $(r-1)(3r+2) \mid d$ then
    28→  $D \leq \frac{2(r-1)(3r+2)}{2r^2-1} \cdot \frac{n}{d} + O(1)$
    29→- If $G$ contains no $K_{2r+1}$ and $(3r-1) \mid d$ then
    30→  $D \leq \frac{3r-1}{r} \cdot \frac{n}{d} + O(1)$
    31→
    32→The first case was disproven for $r \geq 2$ by Czabarka, Singgih, and Székely [CSS21].
    33→
    34→The amended conjecture (due to [CSS21]) states that if $G$ contains no $K_{k+1}$ then
    35→$D \leq (3 - 2/k) \cdot n/d + O(1)$.
    36→
    37→This is known:
    38→- For $k = 2$ ($K_3$-free), proved in [EPPT89]
    39→- For $3$-colourable graphs (weaker than $K_4$-free), by Czabarka, Dankelmann,
    40→  Székely [CDS09]
    41→- For $4$-colourable graphs (weaker than $K_5$-free), by Czabarka, Smith,
    42→  Székely [CSS23]
    43→
    44→It is also known that any connected graph on $n$ vertices with minimum degree $d$ has
    45→$D \leq 3n/(d+1) + O(1)$.
    46→
    47→[EPPT89] Erdős, P., Pach, J., Pollack, R., and Tuza, Zs., _Radius, diameter, and
    48→minimum degree_. J. Combin. Theory Ser. B 47 (1989), 73-79.
    49→
    50→[CSS21] Czabarka, É., Singgih, O., and Székely, L.A., _Counterexample to a conjecture
    51→of Erdős, Pach, Pollack, and Tuza on diameter and minimum degree_. 2021.
    52→
    53→[CDS09] Czabarka, É., Dankelmann, P., and Székely, L.A., _Diameter and degree
    54→conditions for $K_{t,t}$-free graphs_. 2009.
    55→
    56→[CSS23] Czabarka, É., Smith, S.J., and Székely, L.A., _Diameter bounds for
    57→4-colourable graphs_. 2023.
    58→-/
    59→
    60→open SimpleGraph
    61→
    62→namespace Erdos612
    63→
    64→/--
    65→**Erdős Problem 612** (Amended conjecture, [CSS21]):
    66→
    67→If $G$ is a connected $K_{k+1}$-free graph on $n$ vertices with minimum degree $d \geq 1$,
    68→then
    69→$$\operatorname{diam}(G) \leq \left(3 - \frac{2}{k}\right) \cdot \frac{n}{d} + C$$
    70→for some constant $C$ depending only on $k$.
    71→-/
    72→@[category research open, AMS 5]
    73→theorem erdos_612 (k : ℕ) (hk : k ≥ 2) :
    74→    ∃ C : ℝ, ∀ (n : ℕ) (G : SimpleGraph (Fin n)) [DecidableRel G.Adj],
    75→      G.Connected →
    76→      G.CliqueFree (k + 1) →
    77→      G.minDegree ≥ 1 →
    78→      (G.diam : ℝ) ≤ (3 - 2 / (k : ℝ)) * ((n : ℝ) / (G.minDegree : ℝ)) + C := by
    79→  sorry
    80→
    81→/--
    82→**Erdős Problem 612** (Known case $k = 2$, [EPPT89]):
    83→
    84→If $G$ is a connected triangle-free graph on $n$ vertices with minimum degree $d \geq 1$,
    85→then
    86→$$\operatorname{diam}(G) \leq 2 \cdot \frac{n}{d} + C$$
    87→for some absolute constant $C$.
    88→-/
    89→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    90→theorem erdos_612.variants.triangle_free :
    91→    ∃ C : ℝ, ∀ (n : ℕ) (G : SimpleGraph (Fin n)) [DecidableRel G.Adj],
    92→      G.Connected →
    93→      G.CliqueFree 3 →
    94→      G.minDegree ≥ 1 →
    95→      (G.diam : ℝ) ≤ 2 * ((n : ℝ) / (G.minDegree : ℝ)) + C := by
    96→  sorry
    97→
    98→/--
    99→Known general bound ([EPPT89]):
   100→
   101→Any connected graph on $n$ vertices with minimum degree $d \geq 1$ has
   102→$$\operatorname{diam}(G) \leq \frac{3n}{d+1} + O(1).$$
   103→-/
   104→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
   105→theorem erdos_612.variants.general_bound :
   106→    ∃ C : ℝ, ∀ (n : ℕ) (G : SimpleGraph (Fin n)) [DecidableRel G.Adj],
   107→      G.Connected →
   108→      G.minDegree ≥ 1 →
   109→      (G.diam : ℝ) ≤ 3 * ((n : ℝ) / ((G.minDegree : ℝ) + 1)) + C := by
   110→  sorry
   111→
   112→end Erdos612
   113→

    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 182
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/182](https://www.erdosproblems.com/182)
    23→
    24→Let $k \geq 3$. What is the maximum number of edges that a graph on $n$ vertices can
    25→contain if it does not have a $k$-regular subgraph? Is it $\ll n^{1+o(1)}$?
    26→
    27→Asked by Erdős and Sauer [Er75] [Er81]. Resolved by Janzer and Sudakov [JaSu23], who proved
    28→that there exists some $C = C(k) > 0$ such that any graph on $n$ vertices with at
    29→least $C \cdot n \cdot \log(\log(n))$ edges contains a $k$-regular subgraph.
    30→
    31→A construction due to Pyber, Rödl, and Szemerédi [PRS95] shows that this
    32→bound is best possible up to the value of the constant $C(k)$.
    33→
    34→[Er75] Erdős, P., _Problems and results on combinatorial number theory III_,
    35→  Number Theory Day (1975).
    36→
    37→[Er81] Erdős, P., _On the combinatorial problems which I would most like to see solved_,
    38→  Combinatorica 1 (1981), 25–42.
    39→
    40→[JaSu23] Janzer, O. and Sudakov, B., _Resolution of the Erdős–Sauer conjecture on
    41→  regular subgraphs_, 2023.
    42→
    43→[PRS95] Pyber, L., Rödl, V., and Szemerédi, E., _Dense graphs without 3-regular
    44→  subgraphs_, J. Combin. Theory Ser. B 63 (1995), 41–54.
    45→-/
    46→
    47→open SimpleGraph
    48→
    49→namespace Erdos182
    50→
    51→/-- $G$ contains a $k$-regular subgraph: there exists a nonempty finite graph $H$ that
    52→is $k$-regular (every vertex has degree exactly $k$) and admits an injective
    53→graph homomorphism into $G$. -/
    54→def ContainsKRegularSubgraph {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) (k : ℕ) : Prop :=
    55→  ∃ (U : Type) (fU : Fintype U) (_ : Nonempty U) (H : SimpleGraph U) (dH : DecidableRel H.Adj),
    56→    haveI := fU; haveI := dH;
    57→    (∀ v : U, H.degree v = k) ∧
    58→    ∃ f : U → V, Function.Injective f ∧ ∀ u v : U, H.Adj u v → G.Adj (f u) (f v)
    59→
    60→/--
    61→Erdős Problem 182 (Erdős–Sauer conjecture) [Er75] [Er81]:
    62→
    63→For every $k \geq 3$, there exists a constant $C > 0$ such that for all sufficiently
    64→large $n$, every simple graph on $n$ vertices with at least $C \cdot n \cdot \log(\log(n))$
    65→edges contains a $k$-regular subgraph.
    66→
    67→Proved by Janzer and Sudakov [JaSu23].
    68→-/
    69→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    70→theorem erdos_182 :
    71→    ∀ k : ℕ, 3 ≤ k →
    72→    ∃ C : ℝ, 0 < C ∧ ∃ n₀ : ℕ,
    73→    ∀ (n : ℕ), n₀ ≤ n →
    74→    ∀ (G : SimpleGraph (Fin n)) (dG : DecidableRel G.Adj),
    75→      haveI := dG;
    76→      (G.edgeFinset.card : ℝ) ≥ C * (n : ℝ) * Real.log (Real.log (n : ℝ)) →
    77→      ContainsKRegularSubgraph G k := by
    78→  sorry
    79→
    80→end Erdos182
    81→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 64
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/64](https://www.erdosproblems.com/64)
    23→-/
    24→
    25→namespace Erdos64
    26→
    27→/--
    28→Does every finite graph with minimum degree at least $3$
    29→contain a cycle of length $2^k$ for some $k \geq 2$?
    30→-/
    31→@[category research open, AMS 5]
    32→theorem erdos_64 :

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 63
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/63](https://www.erdosproblems.com/63)
    23→
    24→Does every graph with infinite chromatic number contain a cycle of length $2^n$ for
    25→infinitely many $n$? Proved by Liu and Montgomery [LiMo20].
    26→
    27→[Er93,Er94b,Er95,Er95d,Er96,Er97b] Various papers by Erdős where this problem appears.
    28→
    29→[LiMo20] Liu, H. and Montgomery, R., _A solution to Erdős and Hajnal's odd cycle problem_, 2020.

    32→open SimpleGraph
    33→
    34→namespace Erdos63
    35→
    36→/--
    37→Erdős Problem 63 (Conjectured by Mihók and Erdős [Er93,Er94b,Er95,Er95d,Er96,Er97b]):
    38→Does every graph with infinite chromatic number contain a cycle of length $2^n$ for
    39→infinitely many $n$? Proved by Liu and Montgomery [LiMo20].
    40→
    41→Formalized as: for every graph $G$ with infinite chromatic number, for every bound $N$,
    42→there exists $n \geq N$ such that $G$ contains a cycle of length $2^n$.
    43→-/

    45→theorem erdos_63 : answer(True) ↔
    46→    ∀ (V : Type*) (G : SimpleGraph V),
    47→      G.chromaticNumber = ⊤ →
    48→        ∀ N : ℕ, ∃ n : ℕ, N ≤ n ∧
    49→          ∃ v : V, ∃ p : G.Walk v v, p.IsCycle ∧ p.length = 2 ^ n := by
    50→  sorry

    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 62
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/62](https://www.erdosproblems.com/62)
    23→
    24→If $G_1$ and $G_2$ are graphs with chromatic number $\aleph_1$, must they share a common
    25→subgraph with infinite chromatic number?
    26→
    27→[EHS74] Erdős, P., Hajnal, A., and Shelah, S., *On some general properties of chromatic numbers*.
    28→Topics in topology (Proc. Colloq., Keszthely, 1972), Colloq. Math. Soc. Janos Bolyai (1974),
    29→243-255.
    30→
    31→[Er87] Erdős, P., *Some problems on finite and infinite graphs*.
    32→-/
    33→
    34→open SimpleGraph Cardinal
    35→
    36→namespace Erdos62
    37→
    38→/-- A graph $G$ has chromatic number $\aleph_1$ if it cannot be properly colored by any
    39→countable set of colors, but can be colored by a set of cardinality $\aleph_1$. -/
    40→def HasChromaticNumberAleph1 {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) : Prop :=
    41→  (∀ (α : Type*) [Countable α], IsEmpty (G.Coloring α)) ∧
    42→  (∃ α : Type*, #α = aleph 1 ∧ Nonempty (G.Coloring α))
    43→
    44→/-- $G$ contains $H$ as a subgraph via an injective adjacency-preserving map. -/
    45→def ContainsSubgraph {V U : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) (H : SimpleGraph U) : Prop :=
    46→  ∃ f : U → V, Function.Injective f ∧ ∀ u v : U, H.Adj u v → G.Adj (f u) (f v)
    47→
    48→/--
    49→Erdős Problem 62 (strong version) [Er87]:
    50→
    51→If $G_1$, $G_2$ are two graphs with chromatic number $\aleph_1$, must there exist a graph $H$
    52→with infinite chromatic number ($\chi \geq \aleph_0$) which is a subgraph of both $G_1$ and $G_2$?
    53→
    54→This is the stronger form of the conjecture. Erdős also asked [Er87] about
    55→finding such a common subgraph in any finite collection of graphs with
    56→chromatic number $\aleph_1$.
    57→-/
    58→@[category research open, AMS 5]
    59→theorem erdos_62 : answer(sorry) ↔
    60→    ∀ (V₁ : Type*) (V₂ : Type*) (G₁ : SimpleGraph V₁) (G₂ : SimpleGraph V₂),
    61→    HasChromaticNumberAleph1 G₁ →
    62→    HasChromaticNumberAleph1 G₂ →
    63→    ∃ (U : Type*) (H : SimpleGraph U),
    64→      H.chromaticNumber = ⊤ ∧
    65→      ContainsSubgraph G₁ H ∧
    66→      ContainsSubgraph G₂ H := by
    67→  sorry
    68→
    69→/--
    70→Erdős Problem 62 (weak version) [Er87]:
    71→
    72→If $G_1$, $G_2$ are two graphs with chromatic number $\aleph_1$, must there exist a graph $H$
    73→with chromatic number at least $4$ which is a subgraph of both $G_1$ and $G_2$?
    74→
    75→Every graph with chromatic number $\aleph_1$ contains all sufficiently large odd
    76→cycles (chromatic number $3$), proved by Erdős, Hajnal, and Shelah [EHS74].
    77→Erdős wrote [Er87] that 'probably' every graph with chromatic number $\aleph_1$
    78→contains as subgraphs all graphs with chromatic number $4$ with sufficiently
    79→large girth.
    80→-/
    81→@[category research open, AMS 5]
    82→theorem erdos_62.variants.weak : answer(sorry) ↔
    83→    ∀ (V₁ : Type*) (V₂ : Type*) (G₁ : SimpleGraph V₁) (G₂ : SimpleGraph V₂),
    84→    HasChromaticNumberAleph1 G₁ →
    85→    HasChromaticNumberAleph1 G₂ →
    86→    ∃ (U : Type*) (H : SimpleGraph U),
    87→      ¬ H.Colorable 3 ∧
    88→      ContainsSubgraph G₁ H ∧
    89→      ContainsSubgraph G₂ H := by
    90→  sorry
    91→
    92→end Erdos62
    93→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 740
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/740](https://www.erdosproblems.com/740)
    23→
    24→A question of Erdős and Hajnal [Er69b][Er71, p.100][Er81][Er95d].
    25→
    26→Let $\mathfrak{m}$ be an infinite cardinal and $G$ be a graph with chromatic number
    27→$\mathfrak{m}$. Let $r \geq 1$. Must $G$ contain a subgraph of chromatic number
    28→$\mathfrak{m}$ which does not contain any odd cycle of length $\leq r$?
    29→
    30→Rödl proved this is true if $\mathfrak{m} = \aleph_0$ and $r = 3$ (see [108] for
    31→the finitary version).
    32→
    33→More generally, Erdős and Hajnal asked whether for every cardinal $\mathfrak{m}$ and
    34→integer $r$, there exists $f_r(\mathfrak{m})$ such that every graph with chromatic
    35→number $\geq f_r(\mathfrak{m})$ contains a subgraph with chromatic number
    36→$\mathfrak{m}$ with no odd cycle of length $\leq r$.
    37→-/
    38→
    39→open SimpleGraph Cardinal
    40→
    41→universe u
    42→
    43→namespace Erdos740
    44→
    45→/-- The cardinal chromatic number of a graph: the infimum of cardinals $\kappa$
    46→for which $G$ admits a proper $\kappa$-coloring. -/
    47→noncomputable def cardChromaticNumber {V : Type u}
    48→    (G : SimpleGraph V) : Cardinal.{u} :=
    49→  sInf {κ : Cardinal.{u} | ∃ (α : Type u), #α = κ ∧ Nonempty (G.Coloring α)}
    50→
    51→/--
    52→Erdős Problem 740 [Er69b][Er71][Er81][Er95d]:
    53→
    54→If $G$ is a graph with infinite chromatic number $\mathfrak{m}$, then for every
    55→$r \geq 1$, $G$ contains a subgraph with chromatic number $\mathfrak{m}$ that has
    56→no odd cycle of length $\leq r$.
    57→
    58→A question of Erdős and Hajnal. Rödl proved the case
    59→$\mathfrak{m} = \aleph_0$, $r = 3$.
    60→-/
    61→@[category research open, AMS 5]
    62→theorem erdos_740 : answer(sorry) ↔
    63→    ∀ {V : Type u} (G : SimpleGraph V) (𝔪 : Cardinal.{u}),
    64→      ℵ₀ ≤ 𝔪 → cardChromaticNumber G = 𝔪 → ∀ (r : ℕ), 1 ≤ r →
    65→        ∃ (W : Type u) (H : SimpleGraph W),
    66→          cardChromaticNumber H = 𝔪 ∧
    67→          (∃ f : W → V, Function.Injective f ∧ ∀ a b, H.Adj a b → G.Adj (f a) (f b)) ∧
    68→          (∀ (w : W) (p : H.Walk w w), p.IsCycle → Odd p.length → r < p.length) := by
    69→  sorry
    70→
    71→end Erdos740
    72→

    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 642
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/642](https://www.erdosproblems.com/642)
    23→
    24→Let $f(n)$ be the maximal number of edges in a graph on $n$ vertices such that
    25→all cycles have more vertices than chords. Is it true that $f(n) \ll n$?
    26→
    27→A chord is an edge between two vertices of the cycle which are not consecutive
    28→in the cycle. A problem of Hamburger and Szegedy.
    29→
    30→Chen, Erdős, and Staton [CES96] proved $f(n) \ll n^{3/2}$. Draganić, Methuku,
    31→Munhá Correia, and Sudakov [DMMS24] improved this to $f(n) \ll n(\log n)^8$.
    32→
    33→[CES96] Chen, G., Erdős, P., and Staton, W., _Proof of a conjecture of
    34→Bollobás on nested cycles_. J. Combin. Theory Ser. B (1996).
    35→
    36→[DMMS24] Draganić, N., Methuku, A., Munhá Correia, D., and Sudakov, B.,
    37→_Cycles with many chords_. (2024).
    38→
    39→[Er97d] Erdős, P., _Some old and new problems in various branches of
    40→combinatorics_. Discrete Math. (1997).
    41→-/
    42→
    43→open SimpleGraph
    44→
    45→namespace Erdos642
    46→
    47→variable {V : Type*} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V]
    48→         {G : SimpleGraph V} [DecidableRel G.Adj]
    49→
    50→/-- The number of chords of a walk in a graph. A chord is an edge of $G$
    51→connecting two vertices that appear in the walk's support but is not
    52→itself an edge traversed by the walk. -/
    53→noncomputable def numChords {u v : V} (p : G.Walk u v) : ℕ :=
    54→  (G.edgeFinset.filter (fun e =>
    55→    (∀ w, w ∈ e → w ∈ p.support.toFinset) ∧ e ∉ p.edges.toFinset)).card
    56→
    57→/--
    58→Erdős Problem 642 [CES96][Er97d]:
    59→
    60→There exists a constant $C$ such that for all $n$, every graph $G$ on $n$
    61→vertices in which every cycle has strictly more vertices than chords has at
    62→most $C \cdot n$ edges.
    63→-/
    64→@[category research open, AMS 5]
    65→theorem erdos_642 :
    66→    answer(sorry) ↔
    67→      ∃ C : ℕ, ∀ (n : ℕ) (G : SimpleGraph (Fin n)) [DecidableRel G.Adj],
    68→        (∀ (v : Fin n) (p : G.Walk v v), p.IsCycle →
    69→          p.support.toFinset.card > numChords p) →
    70→        G.edgeFinset.card ≤ C * n := by
    71→  sorry
    72→
    73→end Erdos642
    74→

    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 815
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/815](https://www.erdosproblems.com/815)
    23→
    24→Let $k \geq 3$ and $n$ be sufficiently large. Is it true that if $G$ is a graph
    25→with $n$ vertices and $2n - 2$ edges such that every proper induced subgraph has
    26→minimum degree $\leq 2$ then $G$ must contain a copy of $C_k$?
    27→
    28→Such graphs are called *degree 3 critical*. This conjecture was **disproved** by
    29→Narins, Pokrovskiy, and Szabó [NPS17], who proved that there exist arbitrarily
    30→large such graphs with no cycle of length $23$.
    31→
    32→It remains open whether the answer is affirmative when restricted to even $k$.
    33→
    34→[NPS17] Narins, L., Pokrovskiy, A. and Szabó, T., _Graphs without long cycles_, 2017.
    35→-/
    36→
    37→open Filter SimpleGraph
    38→
    39→namespace Erdos815
    40→
    41→/-- A graph on `Fin n` is *degree 3 critical* if it has $2n - 2$ edges and every
    42→    proper induced subgraph has a vertex of degree at most $2$. -/
    43→noncomputable def IsDegree3Critical {n : ℕ} (G : SimpleGraph (Fin n)) : Prop :=
    44→  G.edgeSet.ncard = 2 * n - 2 ∧
    45→  ∀ S : Finset (Fin n), S.Nonempty → S ⊂ Finset.univ →
    46→    ∃ v ∈ S, ((↑S : Set (Fin n)) ∩ G.neighborSet v).ncard ≤ 2
    47→
    48→/--
    49→**Erdős Problem 815** (disproved by Narins–Pokrovskiy–Szabó [NPS17]):
    50→
    51→Is it true that for every $k \geq 3$, every sufficiently large degree 3
    52→critical graph contains a cycle of length $k$? The answer is **no**.
    53→-/
    54→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    55→theorem erdos_815 : answer(False) ↔
    56→    ∀ k : ℕ, k ≥ 3 → ∀ᶠ n in atTop,
    57→      ∀ (G : SimpleGraph (Fin n)), IsDegree3Critical G →
    58→        ∃ (v : Fin n) (p : G.Walk v v), p.IsCycle ∧ p.length = k := by
    59→  sorry
    60→
    61→end Erdos815
    62→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 934
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/934](https://www.erdosproblems.com/934)
    23→
    24→Let $h_t(d)$ be minimal such that every graph $G$ with $h_t(d)$ edges and
    25→maximal degree $\leq d$ contains two edges whose shortest path between them
    26→has length $\geq t$.
    27→
    28→Estimate $h_t(d)$.
    29→
    30→A problem of Erdős and Nešetřil [Er88]. Known:
    31→- $h_t(d) \leq 2d^t$ always and $h_1(d) = d + 1$
    32→- $h_2(d) \leq \frac{5}{4}d^2 + 1$ (Chung–Gyárfás–Tuza–Trotter [CGTT90])
    33→- $h_t(d) \leq \frac{3}{2}d^t + 1$ for all $t \geq 1$ [CCJK22]
    34→
    35→Cambie–Cames van Batenburg–de Joannis de Verclos–Kang [CCJK22] conjecture
    36→that, for all $t \geq 3$, $h_t(d) \sim d^t$ as $d \to \infty$:
    37→$h_t(d) \leq (1 + o(1))d^t$ for all $d$ and
    38→$h_t(d) \geq (1 - o(1))d^t$ for infinitely many $d$.
    39→
    40→[Er88] Erdős, P. and Nešetřil, J., _Irregularities of partitions_ (1989).
    41→
    42→[CGTT90] Chung, F.R.K., Gyárfás, A., Tuza, Z., and Trotter, W.T., _The maximum number of edges
    43→in $2K_2$-free graphs of bounded degree_. Discrete Mathematics (1990).
    44→
    45→[CCJK22] Cambie, S., Cames van Batenburg, W., de Joannis de Verclos, R., and Kang, R.J.,
    46→_Packing graphs of bounded codegree_ (2022).
    47→-/
    48→
    49→open SimpleGraph Classical
    50→
    51→namespace Erdos934
    52→
    53→/-- Two edges $e_1$, $e_2$ in a simple graph $G$ are at distance at least $t$:
    54→for every endpoint $u$ of $e_1$ and $v$ of $e_2$ that lie in the same connected
    55→component, $t \leq \operatorname{dist}_G(u, v)$. (Endpoints in different components are
    56→considered infinitely far apart and satisfy any finite distance bound.) -/
    57→def EdgesAtDist {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) (e₁ e₂ : Sym2 V) (t : ℕ) : Prop :=
    58→  ∀ u, u ∈ e₁ → ∀ v, v ∈ e₂ → G.Reachable u v → t ≤ G.dist u v
    59→
    60→/-- Erdős Problem 934, upper bound direction of the asymptotic conjecture [CCJK22]:
    61→For all $t \geq 3$ and $\varepsilon > 0$, for all sufficiently large $d$, every finite graph
    62→with maximum degree $\leq d$ and more than $(1 + \varepsilon) \cdot d^t$ edges contains two
    63→distinct edges at distance $\geq t$. -/
    64→@[category research open, AMS 5]
    65→theorem erdos_934 :
    66→    ∀ t : ℕ, 3 ≤ t →
    67→    ∀ ε : ℝ, 0 < ε →
    68→    ∃ D₀ : ℕ, ∀ d : ℕ, D₀ ≤ d →
    69→    ∀ (V : Type*) [Fintype V] (G : SimpleGraph V),
    70→      G.maxDegree ≤ d →
    71→      (G.edgeFinset.card : ℝ) > (1 + ε) * (d : ℝ) ^ t →
    72→      ∃ e₁ ∈ G.edgeSet, ∃ e₂ ∈ G.edgeSet,
    73→        e₁ ≠ e₂ ∧ EdgesAtDist G e₁ e₂ t := by
    74→  sorry
    75→
    76→/-- Erdős Problem 934, lower bound direction of the asymptotic conjecture [CCJK22]:
    77→For all $t \geq 3$ and $\varepsilon > 0$, there are infinitely many $d$ for which some graph
    78→with maximum degree $\leq d$ and at least $(1 - \varepsilon) \cdot d^t$ edges has no pair of
    79→distinct edges at distance $\geq t$. -/
    80→@[category research open, AMS 5]
    81→theorem erdos_934.variants.lower_bound :
    82→    ∀ t : ℕ, 3 ≤ t →
    83→    ∀ ε : ℝ, 0 < ε →
    84→    ∀ D₀ : ℕ, ∃ d : ℕ, D₀ ≤ d ∧
    85→    ∃ (n : ℕ) (G : SimpleGraph (Fin n)),
    86→      G.maxDegree ≤ d ∧
    87→      (G.edgeFinset.card : ℝ) ≥ (1 - ε) * (d : ℝ) ^ t ∧
    88→      ¬∃ e₁ ∈ G.edgeSet, ∃ e₂ ∈ G.edgeSet,
    89→        e₁ ≠ e₂ ∧ EdgesAtDist G e₁ e₂ t := by
    90→  sorry
    91→
    92→end Erdos934
    93→

     6→You may obtain a copy of the License at
     7→
     8→    https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
     9→
    10→Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
    11→distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
    12→WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    13→See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→import Mathlib.Combinatorics.SimpleGraph.Clique
    18→import Mathlib.Combinatorics.SimpleGraph.Connectivity.Connected
    19→
    20→/-!
    21→Dominating sets and domination numbers
    22→
    23→This file introduces dominating sets and related invariants.
    24→
    25→Main definitions
    26→
    27→* `SimpleGraph.IsDominating`   : A set of vertices that dominates all vertices.
    28→* `SimpleGraph.IsNDominatingSet` : A dominating set with `n` vertices.
    29→* `SimpleGraph.dominationNumber` : The domination number of a graph.
    30→* `SimpleGraph.IsTotalDominating` : A total dominating set.
    31→* `SimpleGraph.IsTotalNDominatingSet` : A total dominating set with `n` vertices.
    32→* `SimpleGraph.totalDominationNumber` : The total domination number.
    33→
    34→Future work should extend this file with connected, independent, and power
    35→variants as well as domination-related lemmas.
    36→-/
    37→
    38→namespace SimpleGraph
    39→
    40→variable {α : Type*} {G : SimpleGraph α}
    41→
    42→/-! ### Dominating sets -/
    43→
    44→/-- A set `D` is a dominating set for `G` if every vertex of `G` is either in
    45→`D` or adjacent to a vertex of `D`. -/
    46→def IsDominating (G : SimpleGraph α) (D : Set α) : Prop :=
    47→  ∀ v, v ∈ D ∨ ∃ w ∈ D, G.Adj v w
    48→
    49→/-- An `n`-dominating set is a dominating set with `n` vertices. -/
    50→@[mk_iff]
    51→structure IsNDominatingSet (n : ℕ) (D : Finset α) : Prop where
    52→  isDominating : G.IsDominating D
    53→  card_eq : D.card = n
    54→
    55→/-! ### Domination number -/
    56→
    57→/-- The domination number of a graph `G` is the minimum size of a dominating
    58→set. It is `0` if there are no vertices. -/
    59→noncomputable def dominationNumber (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℕ :=
    60→  sInf {n | ∃ D : Finset α, G.IsNDominatingSet n D}
    61→
    62→/-! ### Total domination -/
    63→
    64→/-- A set `D` is a total dominating set if every vertex is adjacent to a vertex
    65→in `D`. -/
    66→def IsTotalDominating (G : SimpleGraph α) (D : Set α) : Prop :=
    67→  ∀ v, ∃ w ∈ D, G.Adj v w
    68→
    69→/-- An `n`-total dominating set is a total dominating set with `n` vertices. -/
    70→@[mk_iff]
    71→structure IsTotalNDominatingSet (n : ℕ) (D : Finset α) : Prop where
    72→  isTotalDominating : G.IsTotalDominating D
    73→  card_eq : D.card = n
    74→
    75→/-- The total domination number of `G`. -/
    76→noncomputable def totalDominationNumber (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℕ :=
    77→  sInf {n | ∃ D : Finset α, G.IsTotalNDominatingSet n D}
    78→
    79→/-! ### Connected domination -/
    80→
    81→/-- A set is a connected dominating set if it is dominating and induces a
    82→connected subgraph. -/
    83→def IsConnectedDominating (G : SimpleGraph α) (D : Set α) : Prop :=
    84→  G.IsDominating D ∧ (G.induce D).Connected
    85→
    86→/-- The connected domination number of `G`. -/
    87→noncomputable def connectedDominationNumber (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℕ :=
    88→  sInf {n | ∃ D : Finset α, G.IsConnectedDominating (D : Set α) ∧ D.card = n}
    89→
    90→/-! ### Independent domination -/
    91→
    92→def IsIndepDominating (G : SimpleGraph α) (D : Set α) : Prop :=
    93→  G.IsIndepSet D ∧ G.IsDominating D
    94→
    95→@[mk_iff]
    96→structure IsNIndepDominatingSet (n : ℕ) (D : Finset α) : Prop where
    97→  isIndep : G.IsIndepSet D
    98→  isDominating : G.IsDominating D
    99→  card_eq : D.card = n
   100→
   101→noncomputable def indepDominationNumber (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℕ :=
   102→  sInf {n | ∃ D : Finset α, G.IsNIndepDominatingSet n D}
   103→
   104→/-! ### Vertex and edge covers -/
   105→
   106→/-- A set of edges is an edge cover if every vertex is incident to some edge in it. -/
   107→def IsEdgeCover (G : SimpleGraph α) (M : Set (Sym2 α)) : Prop :=
   108→  M ⊆ G.edgeSet ∧ ∀ v, ∃ e ∈ M, v ∈ e
   109→
   110→/-- The minimum edge cover number of `G`. -/
   111→noncomputable def edgeCoverNumber (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℕ :=
   112→  sInf {n | ∃ M : Finset (Sym2 α), G.IsEdgeCover (M : Set (Sym2 α)) ∧ M.card = n}
   113→
   114→/-! ### Edge domination -/
   115→
   116→def edgesAdjacent (e e' : Sym2 α) : Prop := ∃ v, v ∈ e ∧ v ∈ e'
   117→
   118→def IsEdgeDominating (G : SimpleGraph α) (M : Set (Sym2 α)) : Prop :=
   119→  ∀ ⦃e⦄, e ∈ G.edgeSet → e ∈ M ∨ ∃ e' ∈ M, edgesAdjacent e e'
   120→
   121→@[mk_iff]
   122→structure IsNEdgeDominatingSet (n : ℕ) (M : Finset (Sym2 α)) : Prop where
   123→  isDominating : G.IsEdgeDominating (M : Set (Sym2 α))
   124→  card_eq : M.card = n
   125→
   126→noncomputable def edgeDominationNumber (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℕ :=
   127→  sInf {n | ∃ M : Finset (Sym2 α), G.IsNEdgeDominatingSet n M}
   128→
   129→end SimpleGraph
   130→

1→/-
     2→Copyright 2025 The Formal Conjectures Authors.
     3→
     4→Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
     5→you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
     6→You may obtain a copy of the License at
     7→
     8→    https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
     9→
    10→Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
    11→distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
    12→WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    13→See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→import Mathlib.Algebra.Module.NatInt
    18→import Mathlib.Data.ENat.Lattice
    19→import Mathlib.Data.Set.Card
    20→import Mathlib.Tactic.IntervalCases
    21→
    22→open scoped Classical
    23→
    24→/-! # Arithmetic Progressions
    25→
    26→Main definitions:
    27→- `Set.IsAPOfLengthWith (s : Set α) (l : ℕ∞) (a d : α)` : predicate asserting that `s` is the
    28→  set consisting of an arithmetic progression of length `l` (possibly infinite) with first term
    29→  `a` and difference `d`. Useful for cases in which additional conditions need to be applied to
    30→  the individual terms and/or difference.
    31→- `Set.IsAPOfLength (s : Set α) (l : ℕ∞)` : predicate asserting that `s` is the set consisting
    32→  of an arithmetic progression of length `l`, for some some first term and difference.
    33→-/
    34→
    35→variable {α : Type*} [AddCommMonoid α]
    36→
    37→/--
    38→A set $S$ is an arithmetic progression of length $l$ with first term $a$ and difference $d$
    39→if $S = \{a, a + d, ..., a + (l - 1)d\}$, if $l$ if finite, else $S = \{a, a + d, a + 2d, ...\}.
    40→This can be written as `Set.IsAPOfLengthWith a l a d`, where `l : ℕ∞` may take the infinite
    41→value `⊤`.
    42→-/
    43→def Set.IsAPOfLengthWith (s : Set α) (l : ℕ∞) (a d : α) : Prop :=
    44→  ENat.card s = l ∧ s = {a + n • d | (n : ℕ) (_ : n < l)}
    45→
    46→/--
    47→A list version of `Set.IsAPOfLengthWith`. Useful when order preservation is required, for example
    48→when considering images under arbitrary functions.
    49→-/
    50→def List.IsAPOfLengthWith (s : List α) (l : ℕ) (a d : α) : Prop :=
    51→  s = (List.range l).map (fun n ↦ a + n • d) ∨ s = (List.range l).reverse.map (fun n ↦ a + n • d)
    52→
    53→namespace Set.IsAPOfLengthWith
    54→
    55→variable {s : Set α} {l : ℕ∞} {a d : α}
    56→
    57→theorem card (h : s.IsAPOfLengthWith l a d) : ENat.card s = l := h.1
    58→theorem eq (h : s.IsAPOfLengthWith l a d) : s = {a + n • d | (n : ℕ) (_ : n < l)} := h.2
    59→
    60→/-- An arithmetic progression with first term `a` and difference `d` is of length zero if and only
    61→if `s` is empty. -/
    62→@[simp]
    63→theorem zero : s.IsAPOfLengthWith 0 a d ↔ s = ∅ := by simp [IsAPOfLengthWith]
    64→
    65→/-- An arithmetic progression with first term `a` and difference `d` is of length one if and only
    66→if `s` is a singleton. -/
    67→@[simp]
    68→theorem one : s.IsAPOfLengthWith 1 a d ↔ s = {a} := by simp +contextual [IsAPOfLengthWith]
    69→
    70→end Set.IsAPOfLengthWith
    71→
    72→namespace List.IsAPOfLengthWith
    73→
    74→variable {s : List α} {l : ℕ} {a d : α}
    75→
    76→theorem length (h : s.IsAPOfLengthWith l a d) : s.length = l := by
    77→  cases h <;> simp_all
    78→
    79→/-- An arithmetic progression with first term `a` and difference `d` is of length zero if and only
    80→if `s` is empty. -/
    81→@[simp]
    82→theorem zero : s.IsAPOfLengthWith 0 a d ↔ s = [] := by
    83→  simp [IsAPOfLengthWith]
    84→
    85→/-- An arithmetic progression with first term `a` and difference `d` is of length one if and only
    86→if `s` is a singleton. -/
    87→@[simp]
    88→theorem one : s.IsAPOfLengthWith 1 a d ↔ s = [a] := by
    89→  simp [IsAPOfLengthWith]
    90→
    91→end List.IsAPOfLengthWith
    92→
    93→/-- In an abelian additive group `α`, the set `{a, b}` with `a ≠ b` is an arithmetic progression of
    94→length `2` with first term `a` and difference `b - a`. -/
    95→theorem Set.isAPOfLengthWith_pair {α : Type*} [DecidableEq α] [AddCommGroup α]
    96→    {a b : α} (hab : a ≠ b) :
    97→    Set.IsAPOfLengthWith {a, b} 2 a (b - a) := by
    98→  simp [IsAPOfLengthWith]
    99→  rw [Finset.card_insert_of_notMem (by simpa only [Finset.mem_singleton])]
   100→  simp
   101→  refine Set.ext fun x => ⟨fun h ↦ ?_, fun ⟨n, ⟨_, _⟩⟩ ↦ by interval_cases n <;> simp_all⟩
   102→  cases h with
   103→  | inl hl => use 0; simp [hl]
   104→  | inr hr => exact ⟨1, by norm_num, by simp_all⟩
   105→
   106→-- Formalisation note: separate result needed for `ℕ` since this is not covered by
   107→-- the `AddCommGroup` result above.
   108→/-- The set `{a, b} : Set ℕ` with `a < b` is an arithmetic progression of length `2` with
   109→first term `a` and difference `b - a`. -/
   110→theorem Nat.isAPOfLengthWith_pair {a b : ℕ} (hab : a < b) :
   111→    Set.IsAPOfLengthWith {a, b} 2 a (b - a) := by
   112→  let ⟨n, h⟩ := Nat.exists_eq_add_of_lt hab
   113→  simp [Set.IsAPOfLengthWith, h, add_assoc]
   114→  exact Set.ext fun x => ⟨fun a => by aesop, fun ⟨w, ⟨_, _⟩⟩ => by interval_cases w <;> simp_all⟩
   115→
   116→/--
   117→The predicate that a set `s` is an arithmetic progression of length `l` (possibly infinite).
   118→This predicate does not assert a specific value for the first term or the difference of the
   119→arithmetic progression.
   120→-/
   121→def Set.IsAPOfLength (s : Set α) (l : ℕ∞) : Prop :=
   122→  ∃ a d : α, s.IsAPOfLengthWith l a d
   123→
   124→/--
   125→A list version of `Set.IsAPOfLength`. Useful when order preservation is required, for example
   126→when considering images under arbitrary functions.
   127→-/
   128→def List.IsAPOfLength (s : List α) (l : ℕ) : Prop :=
   129→  ∃ a d : α, s.IsAPOfLengthWith l a d
   130→
   131→namespace Set.IsAPOfLength
   132→
   133→open Set.IsAPOfLengthWith
   134→
   135→variable {s : Set α} {l : ℕ∞}
   136→
   137→theorem card (h : s.IsAPOfLength l) : ENat.card s = l := h.choose_spec.choose_spec.1
   138→
   139→theorem eq (h : s.IsAPOfLength l) : ∃ a d : α, s = {a + n • d | (n : ℕ) (_ : n < l)} :=
   140→  ⟨h.choose, h.choose_spec.choose, h.choose_spec.choose_spec.2⟩
   141→
   142→/-- Only the empty set is a finite arithmetic progression of length $0$. -/
   143→@[simp] theorem zero : s.IsAPOfLength 0 ↔ s = ∅ := by simp [Set.IsAPOfLength]
   144→
   145→/-- Only singletons are finite arithmetic progressions of length $1$. -/
   146→@[simp] theorem one : s.IsAPOfLength 1 ↔ ∃ a, s = {a} := by simp [IsAPOfLength]
   147→
   148→/-- If a set is an arithmetic progression of lengths `l₁` and `l₂`, then the lengths are
   149→equal. -/
   150→theorem congr {s : Set α} {l₁ l₂ : ℕ∞}
   151→    (h₁ : s.IsAPOfLength l₁) (h₂ : s.IsAPOfLength l₂) :
   152→    l₁ = l₂ := by
   153→  rw [← h₁.card, h₂.card]
   154→
   155→end Set.IsAPOfLength
   156→
   157→namespace List.IsAPOfLength
   158→
   159→open List.IsAPOfLengthWith
   160→
   161→variable {s : List α} {l : ℕ}
   162→
   163→theorem length (h : s.IsAPOfLength l) : s.length = l := by
   164→  obtain ⟨_, _, h⟩ := h
   165→  exact h.length
   166→
   167→/-- Only the empty list is a finite arithmetic progression of length $0$. -/
   168→@[simp] theorem zero : s.IsAPOfLength 0 ↔ s = [] := by simp [IsAPOfLength]
   169→
   170→/-- Only singletons are finite arithmetic progressions of length $1$. -/
   171→@[simp] theorem one : s.IsAPOfLength 1 ↔ ∃ a, s = [a] := by simp [IsAPOfLength]
   172→
   173→/-- If a list is an arithmetic progression of lengths `l₁` and `l₂`, then the lengths are
   174→equal. -/
   175→theorem congr {s : List α} {l₁ l₂ : ℕ}
   176→    (h₁ : s.IsAPOfLength l₁) (h₂ : s.IsAPOfLength l₂) :
   177→    l₁ = l₂ := by
   178→  rw [← h₁.length, h₂.length]
   179→
   180→end List.IsAPOfLength
   181→
   182→theorem Set.isAPOfLength_pair {α : Type*} [DecidableEq α] [AddCommGroup α] {a b : α} (hab : a ≠ b) :
   183→    Set.IsAPOfLength {a, b} 2 :=
   184→  ⟨a, b - a, Set.isAPOfLengthWith_pair hab⟩
   185→
   186→theorem Nat.isAPOfLength_pair {a b : ℕ} (hab : a < b) :
   187→    Set.IsAPOfLength {a, b} 2 :=
   188→  ⟨a, b - a, Nat.isAPOfLengthWith_pair hab⟩
   189→
   190→/-- The empty set is not an arithmetic progression of positive length. -/
   191→theorem Set.not_isAPOfLength_empty {l : ℕ∞} (hl : 0 < l) :
   192→    ¬Set.IsAPOfLength (∅ : Set α) l :=
   193→  fun h ↦ by simp_all [h.congr <| Set.IsAPOfLength.zero.2 rfl]
   194→
   195→/-- We say that a set `s` is free of arithmetic progressions of length `l` if `s` contains no
   196→non-trivial arithmetic progressions of length `l`. Written as `Set.IsAPOfLengthFree s l`. --/
   197→def Set.IsAPOfLengthFree (s : Set α) (l : ℕ∞) : Prop :=
   198→  ∀ t ⊆ s, t.IsAPOfLength l → l ≤ 1
   199→
   200→/-- Any set is free of arithmetic progressions of length `1`, because such APs are all trivial. -/
   201→theorem Set.isAPOfLengthFree_one (s : Set α) : s.IsAPOfLengthFree 1 := by
   202→  simp [Set.IsAPOfLengthFree]
   203→
   204→/-- Any set is free of arithmetic progressions of length `0`, because such APs are all trivial. -/
   205→theorem Set.isAPOfLengthFree_zero (s : Set α) : s.IsAPOfLengthFree 0 := by
   206→  simp [Set.IsAPOfLengthFree]
   207→
   208→/-- Any non-trivial arithmetic progression cannot be free of arithmetic progressions. -/
   209→theorem Set.IsAPOfLength.not_isAPOfLengthFree {s : Set α} {l : ℕ∞}
   210→    (hs : s.IsAPOfLength l) (hl : 1 < l) : ¬s.IsAPOfLengthFree l := by
   211→  simpa [Set.IsAPOfLengthFree] using ⟨s, le_rfl, ⟨hs, hl⟩⟩
   212→
   213→/--
   214→Define the largest possible size of a subset of $\{1, \dots, N\}$ that does not contain
   215→any non-trivial $k$-term arithmetic progression.
   216→-/
   217→noncomputable def Set.IsAPOfLengthFree.maxCard (k : ℕ) (N : ℕ) : ℕ :=
   218→  sSup {Finset.card S | (S) (_ : S ⊆ Finset.Icc 1 N) (_ : (S : Set ℕ).IsAPOfLengthFree k)}
   219→
   220→theorem Set.IsAPOfLengthFree.maxCard_zero (N : ℕ) : maxCard 0 N = N := by
   221→  simp only [maxCard, Nat.cast_zero, isAPOfLengthFree_zero, exists_const, exists_prop]
   222→  apply IsGreatest.csSup_eq
   223→  refine ⟨⟨Finset.Icc 1 N, Subset.rfl, Nat.card_Icc _ _⟩, ?_⟩
   224→  rintro n ⟨S, hS, rfl⟩
   225→  exact S.card_mono hS |>.trans_eq (Nat.card_Icc _ _)
   226→
   227→theorem Set.IsAPOfLengthFree.maxCard_one (N : ℕ) : maxCard 1 N = N := by
   228→  nth_rw 2 [← maxCard_zero N]
   229→  simp [maxCard, isAPOfLengthFree_one, isAPOfLengthFree_zero]
   230→
   231→/-- A set `A` contains an arithmetic progression of length `k` with difference `d`. -/
   232→def Set.ContainsAP (A : Set α) (k : ℕ) (d : α) : Prop :=
   233→  ∃ a, ∃ s, s ⊆ A ∧ s.IsAPOfLengthWith (k : ℕ∞) a d
   234→
   235→def ContainsMonoAPofLength {κ : Type} [Finite κ] {M : Set α}
   236→    (coloring : M → κ) (k : ℕ) : Prop :=
   237→  ∃ c : κ, ∃ ap : Set M, ((·.1) '' ap).IsAPOfLength k ∧
   238→    ∀ m ∈ ap, coloring m = c
   239→
   240→/--
   241→A function `f : α → β` has a monotone `k`-term arithmetic progression if there exists an
   242→arithmetic progression `l` of length `k` in `α` such that its image under `f` is sorted.
   243→-/
   244→def HasMonotoneAP {β : Type*} [Preorder β] (f : α → β) (k : ℕ) : Prop :=
   245→  ∃ l : List α, l.IsAPOfLength k ∧ (l.map f).Pairwise (· < ·)
   246→
   247→/--
   248→Define the largest possible size of a subset of a finset `s` that does not contain
   249→any non-trivial `k`-term arithmetic progression.
   250→-/
   251→noncomputable def Finset.maxAPFreeCard (k : ℕ) (s : Finset α) : ℕ :=
   252→  (s.powerset.filter fun t : Finset α ↦ (t : Set α).IsAPOfLengthFree k).sup Finset.card
   253→
